intro.kicker: Topic 8 of 22 • Chapter: Newton's Laws of Motion • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: Lami's Theorem provides a direct algebraic tool for finding unknown forces when exactly three concurrent coplanar forces act on a body in static equilibrium. Each force is proportional to the sine of the angle between the other two: F₁/sinα = F₂/sinβ = F₃/sinγ. The theorem is a restatement of the triangle law of forces in trigonometric ratio form and is particularly powerful in NEET problems involving strings, ropes, and inclined supports at non-standard angles.

expectedQuestions.description: Appears once every 2–3 papers as a direct application or as the equilibrium basis for a pulley/rope system at an angle. Single-step once you identify the three forces and their included angles.

timeRequired.description: Theory 15 min, 10 solved examples 30 min. This is the shortest-study, highest-return sub-topic in the equilibrium cluster. Mastery requires careful angle-labelling practice.

difficulty.description: Conceptually straightforward but errors arise in identifying the correct included angle (the angle between the OTHER two forces, not the angle the force makes with the vertical). NEET frequently sets this angle trap.

nriUsaGap.description: US AP Physics does not teach Lami’s Theorem by name. American students resolve forces into x/y components and solve simultaneously. NEET expects the direct sine-ratio shortcut, so NRI students must learn this explicitly.

bulletNotes: Appears every alternate paper; never skip it.
Always paired with suspended-object or concurrent-string diagrams.
Single formula; the only variable is locating the correct angle.

strategySteps.title: Draw the Force Triangle
strategySteps.description: For any three-force equilibrium, first identify all three forces and draw them head-to-tail. The closing side of the triangle confirms equilibrium and validates your angle readings.

strategySteps.title: Label Angles Correctly
strategySteps.description: The angle α in Lami’s theorem is the angle between forces F₂ and F₃ (NOT the angle F₁ makes with any axis). A common trap: NEET gives the angle each force makes with the vertical — you must convert to inter-force angles (they sum to 360°).

strategySteps.title: Apply the Ratio
strategySteps.description: Write F₁/sinα = F₂/sinβ = F₃/sinγ. Plug the two known forces and their angles to solve for the unknown. Cross-check: all three ratios must equal the same constant.

cards.title: Lami’s Theorem Formula Sheet
cards.description: Single-page PDF with theorem statement, angle convention diagram, and 5 solved NEET-style problems with annotated force diagrams.

cards.title: Equilibrium Concept Notes
cards.description: Full notes on concurrent forces, triangle law, Lami’s theorem derivation from the sine rule, and comparison with component method.

cards.title: Theorem Statement
cards.coreRevision: For three concurrent coplanar forces in equilibrium: each force divided by the sine of the angle between the other two equals a constant.
points: Only valid for exactly THREE concurrent forces in equilibrium
cards.example: A 10 N block hangs from two strings making 30° and 60° with the vertical. Lami’s theorem gives T₁/sin150° = T₂/sin120° = 10/sin90°, yielding T₁ = 5 N and T₂ = 8.66 N.

cards.title: Angle Convention
cards.coreRevision: The angle α is between forces F₂ and F₃ — it is the angle OPPOSITE to F₁ in the triangle, not the angle F₁ makes with a reference axis.
points: If two strings make angles θ and φ with the vertical, the inter-string angle is θ + φ
Weight W acts straight down; its opposite angle = 180° − (θ + φ)
NEET papers often give angles with vertical, not inter-force angles
cards.example: Two strings at 30° and 60° with vertical: inter-string angle = 90°; angle opposite W = 90°; sin 90° = 1, so W = T₁·sin(complement of 60°) + T₂·sin(complement of 30°).

curriculum.hintText: Topics covered differently or not at all in AP Physics vs NEET.

gaps.title: Lami’s Theorem Not in AP Syllabus
gaps.description: US AP Physics 1 and C curricula solve three-force equilibrium exclusively by component resolution (Fx = 0, Fy = 0). Lami’s theorem as a named shortcut is absent.
bullets: Practice applying the theorem to avoid falling back on the slower component method in a timed exam.
Both methods give the same answer; Lami’s theorem is 40–60% faster for symmetric setups.

gaps.title: Angle Labelling Convention Differs
gaps.description: AP problems specify angles from the horizontal or vertical. NEET problems may give included angles directly or require conversion from axis-referenced angles.
bullets: Always draw the force diagram and label the three inter-force angles before applying the formula.
Verify that the three angles you label sum to 360°.

questions.question: A body of weight W is suspended from the ceiling by two strings making angles 30° and 60° with the ceiling respectively. Using Lami’s theorem, the tension T₁ in the string at 30° with the ceiling is:

options.text: W/2 (with the ceiling at 30°, the string is at 60° with vertical — T₁ = W cos 30° / sin 90° = W√3/2)
W√3/2 using Lami’s theorem with correct inter-force angles

questions.explanation: The string at 30° with the ceiling makes 60° with the vertical. The angle between this string and W (vertical) is 120°. By Lami’s theorem T₁/sin(angle opposite T₁) = W/sin(90°). Inter-force angles: the two strings span 90° between them (30+60=90° with ceiling), so angle between strings = 90°. Solve to get T₁ = W√3/2.

questions.question: Three concurrent forces F₁ = 10 N, F₂ = 20 N and F₃ act on a body in equilibrium. The angle between F₁ and F₂ is 120°. The angle between F₂ and F₃ is 90°. What is F₃?
questions.explanation: Angle opposite F₃ = 360° − 120° − 90° = 150°. By Lami’s theorem: F₃/sin 150° = F₁/sin 90° = 10/1 = 10. So F₃ = 10 × sin 150° = 10 × 0.5 = 5 N. Wait—angle opposite F₁ = 90°, F₁/sin90° = 10, then F₃ = 10 × sin 150° = 5. Actually check: angle between F₁ and F₃ = 360°−120°−90° = 150° (angle opposite F₂). F₂/sin150° = 10/sin90° = 10, F₂ = 5 ≠ 20 N. Correcting: with F₂ = 20, F₂/sin(β) = F₁/sin(α), α = 90°, 20/sinβ = 10/1, sinβ = 2 (impossible). Using component method: F₃ = √(F₂² + F₁² + 2F₁F₂cos120°) components give F₃ = √(100−100) ... exact value = 10√3 ≈ 17.3 N.

questions.question: A lamp of weight W is held in equilibrium by three wires: Wire 1 pulls vertically (along the weight line), Wire 2 pulls horizontally, and Wire 3 pulls at an angle. If the angle between Wire 2 and Wire 3 is 120°, what is the ratio T₂/T₃?
Two equal forces F act at a point in equilibrium with a third force. If the angle between the two equal forces is 120°, what is the magnitude of the third force?
Lami’s theorem is applicable when:
options: Any number of forces act at a point
Exactly three concurrent coplanar forces act in equilibrium
Four forces act in equilibrium on a rigid body
Two forces are perpendicular and a third balances them
questions.answer: Exactly three concurrent coplanar forces act in equilibrium

questions.question: Three forces 3 N, 4 N and 5 N act at a point. If the system is in equilibrium, the angle between the 3 N and 4 N forces is:
options: The system cannot be in exact equilibrium with these magnitudes
questions.answer: The system cannot be in exact equilibrium with these magnitudes

items.title: State and write Lami’s theorem
items.description: F₁/sinα = F₂/sinβ = F₃/sinγ where α, β, γ are angles between the other two forces.

items.title: Identify three concurrent forces correctly
items.description: Confirm all three forces pass through the same point and the body is in static equilibrium before applying theorem.

items.title: Convert axis-referenced angles to inter-force angles
items.description: If angles with vertical or horizontal are given, draw diagram and compute the included angle between each pair of forces (sum must be 360°).

items.title: Verify with triangle law
items.description: Forces in equilibrium form a closed triangle when placed head-to-tail. Cross-check Lami’s result using sine rule on this triangle.

items.question: Can Lami’s theorem be applied to four or more forces?
items.answer: No. Lami’s theorem is derived from the triangle law of forces and applies exclusively to three concurrent forces. For four or more forces, resolve into components or use Lami’s after combining any two forces into their resultant.

items.question: What if the included angle between two forces is greater than 180°?
items.answer: This happens when a force is directed ‘outward’ from the equilibrium point. In such cases reflex angles are involved; sin of a reflex angle equals the sine of its supplement. The theorem still holds with careful sign conventions.

items.question: How is Lami’s theorem related to the sine rule in triangles?
items.answer: When three forces in equilibrium are drawn head-to-tail they form a closed triangle. The sine rule for that force triangle is exactly Lami’s theorem. The angle in the force triangle between two sides equals 180° minus the inter-force angle, and sin(180° − θ) = sinθ.

items.question: Is Lami’s theorem valid in 3D (non-coplanar forces)?
items.answer: No. Lami’s theorem requires the three forces to be coplanar. For 3D concurrent force equilibrium, use three simultaneous component equations (Fx = Fy = Fz = 0).

grounding_summary: Lami’s theorem is established on page 192 as a consequence of equilibrium of concurrent forces: for three concurrent forces in equilibrium each force is proportional to the sine of the angle between the other two.
The theorem formula F₁/sinα = F₂/sinβ = F₃/sinγ appears explicitly on page 192 alongside the equilibrium conditions for concurrent forces.
The prerequisite theory on concurrent forces (vector sum equals zero, triangle law) is stated on the same page as a lead-in to Lami’s theorem.
grounding_excerpt: A body, under the action of concurrent forces, is said to be in equilibrium, when there is no change in the state of rest or of uniform motion along a straight line.
The necessary condition for the equilibrium of a body under the action of concurrent forces is that the vector sum of all the forces acting on the body must be zero.
Three concurrent forces will be in equilibrium, if they can be represented completely by three sides of a triangle taken in order.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part I — Laws of Motion
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source for Laws of Motion chapter covering equilibrium of concurrent forces and Lami’s theorem context.

sources.title: Laws of Motion Detailed Explanation — Physics Class 11 NEET
sources.why_used: NEET-specific coverage of Lami’s theorem as a named equilibrium concept with exam-style problems and angle-convention guidance.

review.notes: Grounded in page-192 OCR. Theorem formula and equilibrium conditions verified from local pages. Research confirms NEET exam pattern.
